rankedItems.rationale: Specific hardware (6GB VRAM laptop), quantitative comparison (30% faster TG, 1.5x-1.75x speedup), and direct A/B testing against Unsloth baseline. Actionable for resource-constrained users.
rankedItems.title: ByteShape Qwen3.6-35B-A3B: 30% Faster Than Unsloth on 6GB VRAM Laptop
rankedItems.summary: ByteShape CPU-5 quantization (Q4_K_S-4.22bpw, 18.3GB) delivers 30% faster token generation than Unsloth UD-IQ4_XS on a 6GB VRAM RTX 3060 laptop with CPU offload, despite being slightly larger. Tested with 65k context, achieving consistent performance across multiple runs after system reboot.
bullets: 30% faster generation (TG) vs Unsloth UD-IQ4_XS on 6GB VRAM RTX 3060
ByteShape: 18.3GB vs Unsloth: 17.7GB (0.6GB larger, 30% speed gain)
105k context window achievable with Q4_0 Hadamard KV cache
Slightly lower prompt processing (PP) speed but better for interactive use
rankedItems.bottomLine: Concrete speedup metric on consumer hardware; ByteShape quants appear underrated vs imatrix alternatives.

rankedItems.rationale: Concrete release with specific model sizes (50M, 124M, 350M), training data (20B tokens), and reproducible code examples. First in SupraLabs scaling plan signals systematic approach to efficiency.
rankedItems.title: Supra-50M Released: Compact 50M-Parameter Model with Competitive Benchmarks
rankedItems.summary: SupraLabs released Supra-50M, a 50M-parameter causal language model trained on 20 billion tokens of high-quality educational text, achieving competitive results despite being significantly smaller than comparable open models. Includes BASE and INSTRUCT versions with custom byte-level BPE tokenizer trained on 500k documents from fineweb-edu.
bullets: 50M parameters trained on 20B tokens of educational web text
Custom BPE tokenizer from 500k fineweb-edu documents
Llama-style architecture with reproducible inference code provided
Part of SupraLabs Scaling Up Plan (124M and 350M variants announced)
rankedItems.bottomLine: Smallest model in digest with concrete training data and architecture details; validates efficiency-first approach at scale.

rankedItems.rationale: Specific hardware (RTX 3090), concrete speedups (4.40x-4.93x), and two model benchmarks with acceptance rates. DFlash implementation shows measurable inference optimization.
rankedItems.title: BeeLlama v0.2.0: 4.4-4.9x Speedup on RTX 3090 via DFlash Optimization
rankedItems.summary: BeeLlama v0.2.0 achieves 164 tps on Qwen 3.6 27B (4.40x baseline) and 177.8 tps on Gemma 4 31B (4.93x baseline) on single RTX 3090 through optimized DFlash implementation, cleaner prefill handling, and drafter K/V projection caching. Prompt processing speed remains near baseline.
bullets: Qwen 3.6 27B: 164 tps (4.40x speedup) with 62% draft acceptance
Gemma 4 31B: 177.8 tps (4.93x speedup) with 71% draft acceptance
Prompt processing speed near baseline (no regression on prefill)
Supports DFlash GGUFs with upstream architecture
rankedItems.bottomLine: Speculative decoding with DFlash achieves 4-5x speedup without quality loss; practical for production inference.

rankedItems.rationale: Quantitative shootout with wikitext-2 eval (583 chunks), token accuracy percentages (89.4% vs 94%), and exponential error analysis. Shows UD-Q5_K_M wins on quality with minimal speed penalty.
rankedItems.title: Qwen3-Coder Quantization Shootout: UD-Q5_K_M Wins on Quality Despite Size
rankedItems.summary: Quantization comparison on Qwen3-Coder-Next across MXFP4_MOE, Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, and UD-Q5_K_M using wikitext-2 evaluation (583 chunks, ctx 512) on 3× R9700 PRO. UD-Q5_K_M achieves 94% token accuracy vs 89.4% for MXFP4, translating to 0.21% vs 0.0014% perfect agreement over 100 tokens.
bullets: UD-Q5_K_M: 94% token accuracy vs MXFP4: 89.4% (5% difference compounds to 500× by token 100)
100-token perfect agreement: UD-Q5 (0.21%) vs MXFP4 (0.0014%)
Speed penalty minimal: UD-Q5 within 9% of Q4_K_M on decode (decode-bound for interactive coding)
UD-Q5 only 22% larger than Q4_K_M but significantly better quality
rankedItems.bottomLine: Token accuracy compounds exponentially; UD-Q5_K_M's 5% improvement prevents hallucination cascades in long-form code generation.

rankedItems.rationale: Specific model size (27B), quantization (IQ4_KS, 14.1GB), context window (105k), and perplexity metric (7.4040 ± 0.02773). Addresses 16GB VRAM constraint with measurable quality.
rankedItems.title: Qwen-27B-IQ4_KS: 14.1GB Quantization for 16GB VRAM with 105k Context
rankedItems.summary: Qwen3.6-27B-i1-IQ4_KS quantization (14.1GB) achieves 105k context window on 16GB VRAM NVIDIA GPUs using ik_llama.cpp with Q4_0 Hadamard KV cache. Perplexity: 7.4040 ± 0.02773 over 12 chunks (65k context), 1.5x-1.75x faster than previous IQ4_XS variant with comparable quality.
bullets: 14.1GB model fits 16GB VRAM with 105k context (vs 14.7GB previous variant)
Perplexity: 7.4040 ± 0.02773 (65k context, 12 chunks)
1.5x-1.75x faster than IQ4_XS; eliminates blank output issues
Requires ik_llama.cpp (NVIDIA CUDA/CPU only, no AMD/Metal support)
rankedItems.bottomLine: Smallest 27B variant for 16GB VRAM; perplexity metric confirms quality parity with larger quantizations.

rankedItems.rationale: Concrete ML pipeline with F1 99%, model size (65MB ONNX int8), cost ($5 total API spend), and browser deployment. Demonstrates agentic tool effectiveness.
rankedItems.title: Prompt Injection Detector: F1 99%, 65MB ONNX, Trained via ML-Agent + DeepSeek
rankedItems.summary: Trained prompt injection classifier using ml-intern agent + DeepSeek v4 Flash. DistilBERT model achieves F1 99% on synthetic dataset, quantized to 65MB ONNX int8, runs in browser via Transformers.js v3. Total API cost: <$5 across all agent runs. Trained on deepset/prompt-injections and Shomi28/prompt-injection-dataset.
bullets: F1 score: 99% (DistilBERT, CPU inference optimized)
Model size: 65MB ONNX int8 (browser-deployable)
Total API cost: <$5 (DeepSeek v4 Flash via OpenRouter)
Runs in browser with Transformers.js v3; no server required
rankedItems.bottomLine: Agentic training cost-effective for specialized tasks; F1 99% on security-critical classifier validates approach.

rankedItems.rationale: Technical deep-dive on asymmetric KV cache quantization with precision loss metric (1.3%) and memory savings (>50%). Addresses infrastructure bottleneck with concrete numbers.
rankedItems.title: Asymmetric KV Cache Quantization: 1.3% Precision Loss, 50%+ Memory Savings
rankedItems.summary: Asymmetric KV cache quantization (q8_0 keys, q4_0 values) in llama.cpp saves >50% memory vs f16/f16 with only 1.3% precision loss. Requires compilation flag or ik_llama.cpp; asymmetric combinations (q8_0/q4_0) currently cause CPU offload on CUDA unless FA_ALL_QUANTS enabled.
bullets: Asymmetric q8/q4 KV cache: >50% memory savings vs f16/f16
Precision loss: only 1.3% (confirmed by user eval)
Current limitation: asymmetric combos trigger CPU offload on CUDA without FA_ALL_QUANTS
Workaround: compile with -DGGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS=ON or use ik_llama.cpp
rankedItems.bottomLine: Infrastructure fix unlocks memory efficiency; 1.3% precision cost is negligible for most workloads.

rankedItems.rationale: Specific funding amount ($10.29B) and strategic commitment to open-source over commercialization. Signals sustained competition and resource availability for model development.
rankedItems.title: DeepSeek Secures $10.29B Funding, Commits to Open-Source AI Development
rankedItems.summary: DeepSeek founder Liang Wenfeng announced $10.29 billion financing round with explicit commitment to continue developing open-source AI models rather than pursuing short-term commercialization. Signals sustained investment in open-weight model development and AGI research.
bullets: $10.29B funding round (largest for open-source AI to date)
Founder commits to open-source development over commercialization
Sustained competition for Qwen, Llama, and other open models
Resources enable long-term research vs quarterly revenue pressure
rankedItems.bottomLine: Largest open-source AI funding validates market; sustained competition drives innovation across all models.

rankedItems.rationale: Novel optimization for MoE models on 12GB VRAM: expert-first caching vs layer-first. Concrete speedup (19→26 t/s at 62% hit rate) with measurable break-even (42% hit rate).
rankedItems.title: Expert-First MoE Caching: 12GB VRAM Optimization Boosts 19→26 t/s on RTX 2060
rankedItems.summary: Experimental llama.cpp fork implements expert-first caching for MoE models on 12GB VRAM (RTX 2060). Instead of offloading complete layers to CPU, caches frequently-used experts in VRAM. Achieves 26 t/s at 62% expert hit rate (vs 19 t/s default --n-cpu-moe), with break-even at 42% hit rate. Supports 100k context with Q6 quantization.
bullets: Expert-first caching: 26 t/s at 62% hit rate vs 19 t/s default (37% speedup)
Break-even: 42% expert hit rate (below this, CPU offload faster)
Targets Qwen 35B A3B (8 experts/token) and Gemma 26B A4B
100k context with Q6 quantization on RTX 2060 (12GB VRAM)
rankedItems.bottomLine: Novel MoE optimization for consumer GPUs; expert caching outperforms layer-first offloading on 12GB VRAM.

rankedItems.rationale: Specific model size (4B VLM), training compute (8xH100 for 3 days), and multiple quantization formats (GPTQ, W8A8, FP8, Q4, Q6). Actionable for document extraction.
rankedItems.title: NuExtract3: 4B Open-Weight VLM for Document Extraction, Self-Hostable
rankedItems.summary: NuExtract3, a 4B VLM based on Qwen3.5-4B under Apache-2.0 license, trained on 8xH100 for 3 days for document extraction, Markdown conversion, and structured data extraction. Supports multiple quantizations (GPTQ, W8A8, FP8, Q4, Q6) and runs on 4GB VRAM. Includes Safetensors, GGUF, and MLX weights.
bullets: 4B parameters (Qwen3.5-4B base) trained on 8xH100 for 3 days
Handles PDFs, screenshots, forms, tables, receipts, invoices, multi-page documents
Multiple quantizations: GPTQ, W8A8, FP8, Q4, Q6 (4GB VRAM minimum)
Apache-2.0 license; self-hostable via vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp
rankedItems.bottomLine: Smallest VLM for document extraction; 4GB VRAM requirement enables edge deployment.
